coredata.dc:title: Fe-carbon dots enhance the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity of TiO2@CN heterojunction
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Kang
Sun, Cong
Chen, Zhiqiang
Qu, Hongxia
Xie, Huifang
Zhong, Qin
coredata.dc:description: A multiheterojunction structured photocatalyst FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN was prepared by a facile low temperature anneal method, which exhibits excellent photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. The CDs with abundant electrons and functional groups transferred electrons to active centers more effectively, and the tightly heterogeneous interface between the carbon dots/TiO2 and graphic carbon nitride improves the separation of electro-holes. In addition the coexistence of two types of TiO2 (anatase and rutile) is more advantageous for NRR compared with pure rutile or anatase TiO2. It was confirmed that FeN-CDs is the active site for nitrogen fixation reaction. The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN reached 9.365 mg h−1 gcat -1. Temperature-programmed desorption (N2-TPD) and in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) were performed to prove the efficient adsorption and protonation of N2 over FeN-CDs. A feasible mechanism was proposed for photo-induced charge separation and nitrogen photofixation on FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation
FeN-CDs
Anatase/rutile TiO2 mixed crystal
TiO2/g-C3N4 heterostructure
Protonation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of multi heterojunction on optical properties

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National NaturalScience Foundation of China (No. 51778296).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN (a, b) and HRTEM images of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN (c) and SAED image of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN (d) and element mapping images of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN (e) and TEM images (f, g) and HRTEM images (h) of FeN-CDs/TiO2.
(a) XRD patterns with different mass loadings and (b) XRD patterns of MXene after introducing FeN-CDs at different calcination temperatures.
XPS spectra: (a) Ti 2p of Ti3C2 and (b) Fe 2p of FeN-CDs/TiO2 compared with iron porphyin and Fe2O3 and (c) N 1 s, (d) C 1 s, (e) Ti 2p of FeN-CDs/TiO2.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectrum and (b) band width and (c) Mott-Schottky test and (d) band structures and (e) electrochemical impedance (EIS) and (f) transient light current response.
Ammonia yield of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN: (a) under N2 and (b) under N2 VS under Ar and (c) cycle stability test and (d) effect of heat treatment temperature.
(a) N2-TPD and (b) nitrogen fixation performance tests at different active sites.
Schematic presentation of electron-hole transfer by FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN photocatalyst.
In situ DRIFTS spectra of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN under N2 and H2O atmospheres.
Illustration of the FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN synthesis route.

head.ce:title: Fe-carbon dots enhance the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity of TiO2@CN heterojunction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Biological Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Biological Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210094, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multiheterojunction structured photocatalyst FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN was prepared by a facile low temperature anneal method, which exhibits excellent photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity. The CDs with abundant electrons and functional groups transferred electrons to active centers more effectively, and the tightly heterogeneous interface between the carbon dots/TiO2 and graphic carbon nitride improves the separation of electro-holes. In addition the coexistence of two types of TiO2 (anatase and rutile) is more advantageous for NRR compared with pure rutile or anatase TiO2. It was confirmed that FeN-CDs is the active site for nitrogen fixation reaction. The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN reached 9.365 mg h−1 gcat -1. Temperature-programmed desorption (N2-TPD) and in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) were performed to prove the efficient adsorption and protonation of N2 over FeN-CDs. A feasible mechanism was proposed for photo-induced charge separation and nitrogen photofixation on FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia is one of the most produced compounds in demand in the world today. Currently, industrial nitrogen fixation is generally achieved using the Harber-Bosch process under harsh reaction conditions (15–25 MPa, 300–550 ℃), which consumes 1.4% of the global energy supply each year and releases 2.3 tons of greenhouse gases from the combustion of large amounts of fossil dyes [1,2]. Therefore, in the face of the energy crisis and environmental pollution challenges, it is necessary and important to explore a low energy consumption and green and environmentally friendly method of gentle nitrogen fixation.

In recent years, a green-friendly photo-catalytic nitrogen fixation technology has received great attention [23,24]. Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation is a typical photocatalytic reaction process that uses photocatalysts to respond to sunlight, resulting in the reduction of photo-excitation electrons to nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR) [3–10]. However, the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation mainly face three problems: a) high bond energy of N≡N three bonds; b) poor light absorption; c) restricted charge separation [11]. In response to these problems, many groups have carried out in-depth research. Zhang et al. [12] enhanced the absorption of light and carrier separation by interface engineering over titanium dioxide and achieved excellent nitrogen fixation effect. The face-to-face contact between the two materials can promote the charge transfer across the heterostructure interface, which can improve the electron-hole separation efficiency [54]. Luo et al. [13] put forward the effect of catalyst activity on reactant (N2) and product (NH3) adsorption balance on the reaction for the first time. Xiong et al. [14] doped the molybdenum atoms at the defective point in the W18O49 catalyst as the active point which reduced the activation energy of nitrogen dissociation and achieved the efficient activation of nitrogen molecules in the photocatalytic system. The above research shows that the heterojunctions, doping and defects can solve the above three difficulties in a targeted manner.

Graphite phase nitrided carbon polymer (g-C3N4) as a semiconductor photocatalyst shows a bright future, due to its simple preparation process, high thermal and chemical stability, relatively low band gap energy (Eg) of 2.7 eV, in the field of visible photocatalysis [15–20]. But it also has some problems like poor charge separation efficiency [25], weak adsorption of N2, making the conventional preparation of g-C3N4 photo catalytic NRR very inefficient. It is necessary to optimize g-C3N4. For example, Yao et al. fabricated iodine doped g-C3N4 to reduce its energy band gap, expanding the visible light response range of the catalyst, which in turn increased activity and charge separation efficiency of the catalyst nitrogen fixation g-C3N4 [22]. In addition, the coupling of carbon dots (CDs) with an appropriate semiconductor matrix can not only broaden the photoabsorption range of the semiconductor particles but also promote the separation of photoinduced charge carriers [21].

Herein, we successfully combined nitrogen defect g-C3N4 with Fe-carbon dots modified TiO2 to build an excellent photocatalyst that can be used for nitrogen fixation. The nitrogen defect g-C3N4 is prepared by using citric acid and melamine for super-molecular pre-assembly and re-pyrogenic pre-exorcism which is cheap and environmental-friendly. The introduction of Fe chelating to MXene as a pre-driven body prepares MXene derivatives with FeN-CDs modification. The derivative is used as a nitrogen fixation active substance to establish a multi-composite heterogeneous structure with nitrogen defect g-C3N4. This heterogeneic structure shows considerable photo-catalytic nitrogen fixation.

41.4 mg of iron (Ⅱ) acetate and 300.9 mg of 1,10-phenanthroline were dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol and heated to 60 ℃ for 4 h to get a homogeneous solution. The complex solution was then transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermal reacted for 24 h at 160 ℃. After cooling to room temperature and centrifugation at 8000 rpm, a brown FeN-CDs colloid was obtained (the iron content is about 15.9 mmol L-1).

MXene nanosheet was prepared according to previous report [51]. Take 0.5 mL FeN-CDs colloid mixed with 10 mL MXene nanosheet solution and ultrasound for 2 h under the protection of nitrogen flow to obtain a uniform dispersion suspension. After centrifugation and vacuum drying at 60 ℃, a black solid sample was obtained and then calcined under nitrogen atmosphere at 600 ℃ for 2 h. The final powder was named FeN-CDs/TiO2.

The g-C3N4 (CN) was prepared according to our previous report [51]. A certain amount of FeN-CDs/TiO2 and g-C3N4 were dispersed in 100 mL solutions consisting of 75 mL deionized water and 25 mL isopropyl alcohol and then ultrasound dispersed for 4 h. After centrifugation (4200 rpm) and vacuum drying at 60 ℃, FeN-CDs/TiO2@CNx were prepared, where x represented the weight percent of FeN-CDs/TiO2 to CN, x = 1, 2.5, 5. If there are no special instructions, FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN stands for FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN 2.5. The complete preparation process is shown in Scheme 1 .

The N2 photocatalytic fixation experiments were carry out in the photocatalytic reactor. Briefly, 50 mg of the catalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of 5 wt% aqueous methanol solution, and purity nitrogen gas was bubbled into the reaction solution with a flow rate of 30 mL min−1. After stirring for 30 min in the dark, a 300 W Xe lamp was turned on. At given irradiation time intervals, 2 mL of the reaction solution was taken out and liquid sample was separated by centrifugation at 4200 rpm for 10 min. The concentration of ammonia was determined by Nessler's reagent method (JB7478-87) with UV–visible spectrophotometer (T6). The ammonia concentration was calculated by ammonia nitrogen standard curve (Fig. S6).

The XRD patterns were obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Purkinje XD-3, Cu Kα radiation). The morphological of FeN-CDs/TiO2 and FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN, 300 kV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was recorded on a Nicolet-iS10 spectrometer. The composition and surface state of the samples were verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI QUANTERA II, Al Kα X-ray radiation). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) spectras were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) measurements were performed on a ChemiSorb 2040 instrument. Transient photocurrents, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott–Schottky plots were measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, three-electrode system in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution as an electrolyte). The work electrode was fabricated using a drop-casting method. 5 mg of the photocatalysts were dispersed in 1 mL ethanol containing 40 μL Nafion solution (5%) by sonication for 30 min. Then, the dispersive suspension was drop-cast onto a pretreated fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrate, and dried at 60 ℃ in a vacuum oven for 2 h to improve the adhesion.

The structure and morphology of the FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN were investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 (a, b), the FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN catalyst maintains the original nanosheet morphology of CN (Fig. S1), and the FeN-CDs/TiO2 is densely anchored on the surfaces of CN to form intimate heterojunctions (Fig. 1a). HRTEM images in Fig. 1c further clearly present this multi heterointerfaces of the FeN-CDs, TiO2, and CN: the lattice interplanar spacing of 0.351 nm and 0.248 nm is corresponded to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 and rutile TiO2, respectively, which is consistent with the result of Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) in Fig. 1d. The lattice interplanar spacing of 0.322 nm is corresponded to the (002) plane of CN. In addition, it can be observed the amorphous FeN-CDs is enmeshed in the TiO2 and no lattice fringe relate d to Fe can be found, which could be caused by the formation of FeN species (section 3.2). Obviously, a multi-heterojunction structure consisted of A/R mixcrystalline TiO2, CN nanosheet and FeN-CDs is constructed for FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN, which is beneficial to the faster transfer of photogenerated electrons to the nearby interface and restrain the recombination of electrons and holes. On the one hand, HAADF-STEM image and corresponding STEM–EDS elemental mapping in Fig. 1e reveal the highly uniform dispersion of Fe, N and Ti on FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN which confirms the existence of Fe on the composite catalyst FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN. On the other hand, STEM–EDS elemental maps of FeN-CDs/TiO2 (Fig. S2e) revealed that the Fe and N species are distributed evenly in FeN-CDs/TiO2. In order to reveal the structure of the catalyst more clearly, TEM and HRTEM analysis of Fe-CD/TiO2 without CN are carried out, as shown in Fig. (f-h). There is no doubt that the heterojunctions composed of FeN-CDs and A/R TiO2 can be clearly seen (Fig. 1g) and the (101) plane of rutile and anatase have also been discovered (Fig. 1h).
Moreover, the Ti on MXene is only oxidized in situ, which prevented TiO2 from agglomerating into clumps, so FeN-CDs/TiO2 still retains the original two-dimensional nano flake morphology.

The structure of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN is further confirmed by XRD. Fig. 2 a presents the XRD patterns of CN and FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN with different mass percentage of FeN-CDs/TiO2. All samples show two characteristic peaks at 13.0° and about 27.8° ascribed to (100) and (002) planes representing in-plane packing and inter-facial stacking of g-C3N4 sheets, respectively [31]. Comparing with the pure g-C3N4, the diffraction peak of (002) crystal plane for FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN gradually shifts from 27.8° to 27.6° (Fig. 2a, Fig. S7), indicating that FeN-CDs/TiO2 plays an intercalation role which increased CN’s layer spacing.

To insight the phase transition behavior between anatase TiO2 and rutile TiO2, the precursor FeN-CDs/Mxene (MXene transformed into TiO2 after calcination) was pretreated at different annealing temperature and corresponding results of XRD are displayed in Fig. 2b. As can be seen the characteristic peak at 9.2° assigned to the (002) crystal plane of Ti3C2 MXene (JCPDS No. 32-1383) [50] is significantly reduced with temperature in a nitrogen atmosphere. Meanwhile, new characteristic diffraction peaks at 25.4°, 35.9° and 53.8° attributed to (101), (103) and (204) crystal plane of anatase TiO2 and 27.0° attributed to (110) crystal plane of rutile TiO2, which is consistent with the TEM results and indicates that Ti3C2 is partially oxidized to mixed crystal TiO2. It is worth noting that rutile content increased as anatase content decreased and rutile content increased gradually from 41.15 wt％ (500 °C) to 67.11 wt％ (700 °C) which is calculated by the integral area of the main peak. When the temperature was increased to 800 °C, only rutile TiO2 can be detected while both anatase TiO2 and Ti3C2 MXene completely disappeared, indicating that Ti3C2 MXene has been completely transformed into rutile [26,27]. The pure Ti3C2 still retains the Mxene characteristic peaks below 500 ℃ but almost turns into rutile above 600℃ (Fig. S3). Evidently, calcination may promote the reaction of Ti with its own rich oxygen groups, resulting in the transformation of Ti3C2 to mixed crystal TiO2 in the range of 500–700 ℃ [28,29]. Interestingly, for the photocatalytic reaction, the construction of heterogeneous or homogeneous interface can not only improve the light quantum efficiency, but also the microenvironment formed at the interface can modulate the active site to promote the reaction. The activity experiment results also found that the catalyst with anatase and rutile structure has higher activity than rutile. The effects of interface and titanium dioxide structure on catalyst activity are discussed in section 3.3.

All the samples did not show characteristic peaks related to the iron or iron oxides such as Fe2O3 (30.2°, 35.6°, 43.3°, PDF number 25-1402,) and FeO (60.7°, 61.2°, 72.6°, PDF number 25-1402,)[30], indicating the Fe species is highly dispersed on the surface of CDs/TiO2 due to the ligand protection of phenanthroline which may be likely to exist in the form of FeN, which is consistent with the XPS spectra (Fig. 3 ).

The chemical state and coordination environment of surface elements was investigated by XPS spectroscopy, which is sensitive to the chemical environment of metal elements. To better understand the existence of Fe, the XPS spectra of Fe2O3 and chloro (protoporphyrinato) iron (III) (Hemin, C34H36O4N4Fe) with Fe-N4 structure were also collected. It can be seen from Fig. 3a, the Fe 2p XPS spectrum of FeN-CDs/TiO2 is very similar to that of iron porphyrin but distinctly lower than that observed for the iron oxides, which lower binding energy of Fe compared to Fe2O3 due to the coordination by N rather than that of O. Combined with the results of XRD, HRTEM of FeN-CDs/TiO2, this results provide evidence for the presence of Fe-N active centers in the catalysts. The peak of Fe 2p3/2 for FeN-CDs/TiO2 could be further deconvoluted two binding energies of 710.9 eV and 709.9 eV ascribed to Fe3+ and Fe2+, respectively, this redox circle of Fe3+ ↔ Fe2+ as active center increases the NRR activity [43]. The N 1 s spectra is presented in Fig. 4 c that could be deconvoluted into pyridinic-type nitrogen (BE = 398.2 eV), pyrrolic–nitrogen (BE = 399.9 eV), and graphitic-type nitrogen (BE = 401.3 eV) [37–41]. Pyridinic-type nitrogen and pyrrolic–nitrogen could form Fe-N bond which could transfer protons to N2 [41,42]. The presence of N, which derived from the 1,10-phenanthroline ligand coordinated with Fe, further confirms that the Fe atoms in the FeN-CDs/TiO2 exist in Fe-N coordination environments. The C 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into three C species in Fig. 3d: C-C, C-N and C-O. The existence of C-N is consistent with the results of FTIR (Fig. S8) which is the C-N framework in the nitrogen-doped carbon material obtained by the condensation and carbonization of 1,10-phenanthroline-ferrous complex ion during the hydrothermal and calcination process. The FTIR spectroscopy of FeN-CDs/TiO2 shown that the characteristics peaks observed at around 1190 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1 were due to the stretching of C–N group and at around 1670 cm−1 can be ascribed to C = N group [32–35]. The characteristics peaks observed from 700 to 900 cm−1 was due to the overlapping of several bands attributed to different vibration modes of Ti-O and Ti-O-Ti in TiO2 [36,55]. The Ti 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3e) of FeN-CDs/TiO2 only shows 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks of Ti-O, indicating that Ti3C2 with multiple valence Ti species was oxidized to TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of multi heterojunction on optical properties
ce:section.ce:para:
It is not difficult to find that after being combined with FeN-CDs/TiO2, the light absorption edge of CN has a weak red shift, which may be caused by the introduction of carbon dots in FeN-CDs/TiO2 and the abundant intermediate energy levels in the band gap [44–46] as shown in the ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, Fig. 4a). The band gap was obtained by UV–vis DRS spectra and Kubelka-Munk equation as shown in Fig. 4b. The as-calculated band gap (Eg) of 2.51 eV for FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN is smaller than that of 2.54 eV for CN, this slightly narrowing of the band gap makes it easier for electrons to transition from the valence band to the conduction band, reflecting the enhancement of the light response. For g-C3N4 and FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN, Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 4c) are analyzed to estimate the flat band potential, which is can be seen as the conduction band potential [56,57]. Mott-Schottky plots are analyzed by equations:
1
C
2
=
2
e ε
ε 0
N D
E -
E
f b
-
K T
e
N D
=
2
e ε
ε 0
d
1
C
2
d
E
- 1

Here, C is the differential capacitance of the Helmholtz layer, e represents the charge of single electron (1.602 × 10−19C), ε is the dielectric of CN, ε0 is the vacuum permittivity (8.85 × 10−14F cm−1), E is the applied bias at the electrode, K is the Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10−23 J K−1), and T is the absolute temperature.

Band gap diagram (Fig. 4d) is obtained by Mott-Schottky test (Fig. 4c) and semiconductor energy band structure formula Eg = Ev − Ec. The conduction band potential and valence band potential of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN have moved negatively by 0.15 eV and 0.18 eV compared with CN due to the existence of A/R TiO2 mixed crystals heterojunctions. The more negative conduction band provides more reductive photogenerated electrons (−0.55 → −0.70 eV), which is very beneficial to the nitrogen reduction reaction.

Fig. 4e is the Nyquist plot obtained from the electrochemical impedance (EIS) test of the photocatalyst. It is obvious that due to the carbon dots and the heterostructures, FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN exhibited lower electrical resistance and stronger transient photocurrent response of the photocatalyst (Fig. 4f) than CN.

The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation activity of all the as-prepared photocatalysts was examined under all-light irradiation at 35 °C for 4 h. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN exhibits greatly improved catalytic performance comparing with the CN and FeN-CDs/TiO2 and a highest ammonia yield of 9.365 mg·h−1·gcat -1 was achieved by FeN-CDs/TiO2 2.5, which is about 7 times higher than CN and 3.5 times higher than TiO2@CN. Such excellent catalytic performance can be attributed to Fe-N active center.

As shown in Fig. 5b, when argon was used to replace nitrogen for the reaction, almost No NH3 is formed, which indicates that NH3 is generated by the reduction of N2 instead of nitrogen from catalyst. The cycle stability test results of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN are shown in Fig. 5c. After four cycles of photocatalytic nitrogen fixation experiments, the NH3 yield of the catalyst decreased slightly from 9.365 mg·h−1·gcat -1 to 8.247 mg·h−1·gcat -1, maintaining more than 80% of the catalytic activity. Fig. 5d shows the catalytic performance of the FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN prepared by combining CN with FeN-CDs/TiO2 which is at different calcination temperature. It can be found that FeN-CD/TiO2 at 600 °C has the highest activity which is attributed to the multi-heterojunction (section 3.1).

The FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN 2.5 with the best performance will be discussed further. As shown in Fig. 6 a, it can be found that FeN-CDs/TiO2 has a strong and broad N2 desorption peak after 350 °C, indicating that there are a large number of highly active chemisorption sites on FeN-CDs/TiO2, which is not available in a single CN catalyst. FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN also showed obvious chemical adsorption peaks. Therefore, there are strong nitrogen adsorption activation centers on FeN-CDs/TiO2.

In order to further study the location of active sites on FeN-CDs/TiO2, TiO2 without FeN-CDs and FeO/TiO2 in which FeN-CDs was replaced by Fe2O3 were prepared, and they were combined with CN in the same way. Then the photocatalytic nitrogen fixation performance is tested (Fig. 6b). It is found that TiO2@CN only exhibits about 1/4 of the performance of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN, indicating that only carbon-coated mixed crystal TiO2 lacks a large number of activation sites for nitrogen molecules. The loading of FeO/TiO2 on CN also failed to improve the nitrogen fixation performance, indicating that the Fe-O provided by Fe2O3 nanoparticles did not have the ability to activate nitrogen molecules, and only introducing FeN-CDs/TiO2 can provide sufficient reactivity.

According to section 3.3, N2 would be adsorbed on the catalyst, which is expressed as Eq. (1). According to results of section 3.2 and previous report [49], the electron-hole pairs generated on the surface of the FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN upon absorption of light irradiations are illustrated in Fig. 7 . The combination of A/R TiO2 and g- C3N4 has formed a two-type II semiconductor heterojunction [56], where the CB and VB potentials of g-C3N4, anatase TiO2 and rutile TiO2 are (−0.55 eV, +1.99 eV), (−0.50 eV, +2.7 eV) and (−0.29 eV, +2.81 eV), respectively [49]. The valence band (2.81 eV → 2.7 eV → 1.99 eV) and conduction band (−0.55 eV → -0.5 eV → −0.29 eV) whose absolute value gradually decreases are more conducive to the separation of electrons and holes, which allows electrons to be more easily transferred from the anatase phase to the lower energy level rutile lattice trap sites [47–49]. Under light irradiation, photons with high energies can activate the electrons in the valence band (VB) of g-C3N4 that leap from the VB to the CB of g- C3N4 under visible light as shown in Eq. (2) that also leads to the creation of holes in the VB of g-C3N4. Due to more negative potential the excited electrons from CB of g-C3N4 are quickly transferred to that of rutile TiO2 through anatase TiO2 that are ultimately captured by the FeN-CDs as shown in Eqs. (3)–(5).

In situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) was used to confirm the effect of the FeN-CDs sites on the N2 adsorption and activation (see Fig. S9 for details). As shown in Fig. 8 , several absorption peaks changed with the visible light irradiation time from 0 to 50 min. The peak at 3500 cm−1 corresponds to the N–H stretching mode, and the peak at 1720 cm−1 originates from the characteristic absorption of NH4 +, indicating that the protonation took place on the FeN-CDs during the proceeding DRIFTS measurement expressed as Eq. (6). It is noteworthy that a peak at 1670 cm−1 can be attributed to the chemisorbed N2 [52]. As is well known, the N2 molecules themselves have no signal in DRIFTS spectra owing to their zero dipole moments [53], but active Fe sites on the FeN-CDs donate electrons to antibonding orbitals of chemisorbed dinitrogen molecules, leading to polarization of the N≡N triple bonds.
(1) FeN-CDs + N2 → N2-FeN-CDs
(2) g-C3N4 + hν → g-C3N4(e−) + g-C3N4(h+)
(3) g-C3N4(e−) + A-TiO2 → g-C3N4 + A-TiO2(e−)
(4) A-TiO2(e−) + R-TiO2 → A-TiO2 + R- TiO2(e−)
(5) R-TiO2(e−) + N2-FeN-CDs → R-TiO2 + N2-FeN-CDs(e−)
(6) N2-FeN-CDs(e−) + H+ → NH3 + FeN-CDs

It can be determined that the enhanced photo-catalytic activity of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN was attributed to two main reasons: Firstly, the in-situ formation of TiO2 built an efficient composite that resulted in a fast transfer of electrons and holes leading to efficient photocatalytic activity. Secondly, N2 is adsorbed and polarized by Fe sites, so that it is easier to be protonated to generate NH3.

The multi-heterojunction of FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN can enhance the stability of electron/hole charge separation. Fe with carbon dots is the active part that can greatly improve the nitrogen fixation activity of the catalyst. TiO2 and CN had high electron-donating ability that transferred electron to FeN-CDs for the efficient progress of the nitrogen reduction reaction. The ligand can play a protective role to prevent Fe from agglomerating during the calcination process, so that Fe as the active center can be well dispersed on the surface of the catalyst. The process of nitrogen adsorption, polarization, and protonation over FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN has been confirmed in this work, which would enrich our knowledge on designing and developing photocatalytically active catalysts for nitrogen fixation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National NaturalScience Foundation of China (No. 51778296).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.132440.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable bioenergy production from marginal lands in the US Midwest

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Gelfand, R. Sahajpal, X. Zhang, et al., Sustainable bioenergy production from marginal lands in the US Midwest, Nature 493 (2013) 514–517.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How a century of ammonia synthesis changed the world

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.W. Erisman, M.A. Sutton, J. Galloway, et al., How a century of ammonia synthesis changed the world, Nature Geoscience 2008 (2018) 636–639.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unravelling the electrochemical mechanisms for nitrogen fixation on single transition metal atoms embedded in defective graphitic carbon nitride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, X. Zhao, Z. Kong, W. Ong, N. Li, Unravelling the electrochemical mechanisms for nitrogen fixation on single transition metal atoms embedded in defective graphitic carbon nitride, J. Mater. Chem. A 6 (2018) 21941–21948.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient and selective generation of ammonia and hydrogen on a graphdiyne-based catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Hui L, Y. Xue, H. Yu, Y. Liu, Y. Fang, et al., Highly efficient and selective generation of ammonia and hydrogen on a graphdiyne-based catalyst, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 141 (2019) 10677–10683.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An MnO2–Ti3C2Tx MXene nanohybrid: an efficient and durable electrocatalyst toward artificial N2 fixation to NH3 under ambient conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Kong, F. Gong, Q. Zhou, G. Yu, L. Ji, et al., An MnO2–Ti3C2Tx MXene nanohybrid: an efficient and durable electrocatalyst toward artificial N2 fixation to NH3 under ambient conditions, J. Mater. Chem. A 7 (2019) 18823–18827.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Computational design of Single-Molybdenum catalysts for the nitrogen reduction reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Li, S. Qiu, C. Liu, et al., Computational design of Single-Molybdenum catalysts for the nitrogen reduction reaction, J. Phys. Chem. C 123 (2019) 2347–2352.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Building up a picture of the electrocatalytic nitrogen reduction activity of transition metal single-atom catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, Y. Jiao, Y. Zheng, M. Jaroniec, S. Qiao, Building up a picture of the electrocatalytic nitrogen reduction activity of transition metal single-atom catalysts, J. Am. Chem. Soc 141 (2019) 9664–9672.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Predicting two-dimensional pentagonal transition metal monophosphides for efficient electrocatalytic nitrogen reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ying, K. Fan, X. Luo, H. Huang, Predicting two-dimensional pentagonal transition metal monophosphides for efficient electrocatalytic nitrogen reduction, J. Mater. Chem. A 7 (2019) 11444-11451.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards establishing standard performance metrics for batteries, supercapacitors and beyond

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Noori, M.S. Rahmanifar, R.B. Kaner, M.F. Mousavi, et al., Towards establishing standard performance metrics for batteries, supercapacitors and beyond, Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 1272–1341.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tackling the activity and selectivity challenges of electrocatalysts toward the nitrogen reduction reaction via atomically dispersed biatom catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Guo, J. Gu, S. Lin, S. Zhang, Z. Chen, S. Huang, Tackling the activity and selectivity challenges of electrocatalysts toward the nitrogen reduction reaction via atomically dispersed biatom catalysts, J. Am. Chem. Soc 142 (2020) 5709–5721.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photon-driven nitrogen fixation: current progress, thermodynamic considerations, and future outlook

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Medford, M.C. Hatzell. Photon-driven nitrogen fixation: current progress, thermodynamic considerations, and future outlook, ACS Catal 7 (2017) 2624–2643.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: TiO2 (B) nanotubes with ultrathin shell for highly efficient photocatalytic fixation of nitrogen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wu, X. Tan, K. Liu, J. Lei, L. Wang, J. Zhang. TiO2 (B) nanotubes with ultrathin shell for highly efficient photocatalytic fixation of nitrogen, Catalysis Today 335 (2019) 214–220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ modification of cobalt on MXene/TiO2 as composite photocatalyst for efficient nitrogen fixation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Gao, X. Li, S. Luo, Z. Luo, X. Zhang, R. Huang, M. Luo, In situ modification of cobalt on MXene/TiO2 as composite photocatalyst for efficient nitrogen fixation, Journal of Colloid and Interface Science 585 (2021) 20–29.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Refining defect states in W18O49 by Mo doping: a strategy for tuning N2 activation towards solar-driven nitrogen fixation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Zhang, A. Jalil, D. Wu, S. Chen, Y. Liu, C. Gao, et al., Refining defect states in W18O49 by Mo doping: a strategy for tuning N2 activation towards solar-driven nitrogen fixation, J. Am. Chem. Soc 140 (2018) 9434–9443.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, Y. Liu, N. Liu, Y. Han, et al., Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway, Science 347 (2015) 970–974.
